author: @BanjoLuke1
text:
I'll never know, but I wonder sometimes whether Rains' character was so named because of Louis Renault and his company.
I'm not sure how widely Renault's passive (?) collaboration with the German occupiers was known in Hollywood at the time this film was written/made.
However... Captain Renault lives in Vichy Casablanca in the very same fog of deceit and pretence that Louis Renault used to profit from occupation. 
I may be wrong.
But I do wonder...

text: 오늘은2026년4월25일 토요일 현재시각 오후3시29분 여호와의눈이 온땅을 두루감찰하사 전심으로 자기를 향하는자에게 능력을 베푸시나니라고 말씀하신주님 아버지하나님 캐나다 잠수함사업80조수주를 미국해군군함 구축함 이지스함 잠수함 프릿킷트함 무인잠수정 줌왈트건조 항공모함건조 폴란드잠수함8조수주를 우리 한화오션에게 허락해주십시요 and K9A2차륜형선더볼드자주포가 미국육군자주포가 되게하소서 유럽모델이 되게하소서 and K2A2흑표전차가 중동국가 & 남미국가 & 동남아국가 & 호주 뉴질랜드 & 유럽국가들에게 주력전차가 되게하소서 우리주 예수그리스도의 이름으로 기도드립니다 아멘 감사합니다 나는 전능하신 하나님 천지의 창조주를 믿습니다 나는 그의 유일하신 아들 우리주 예수그리스도를 믿습니다 그는 성령으로 잉태되어 동정녀마리아에게 나시고 본디오빌라도에게 고난을 받으사 십자가에 못받혀 죽으시고 장사된지 삼일만에 죽은자가운데서 다시 살아나셨으며 하늘에 오르사 전능하신 하나님 우편에 앉자계시다가 거기로부터 살아있는자와 죽은자를 심판하려 오시리라 성령을 믿으며 거룩한 공회와성도가 서로 교통하는것과 죄를 사하여 주시는것과 몸이다시사는것과 영원히 사는것을 믿사옵나이다 아멘 감사합니다

author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @J_Ges
text:
Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

author: @zineelabidineelberri984
text: ...La nuit de mon périple à destination de la ville de Rabat ne fit que commencer, lorsqu' un vacarme assourdissant, produit par moments et par endroits aux passages du train dans les tunnels, m'empêcha de m'assoupir pendant tout un trajet nocturne plein d'incertitudes, pour me retrouver abandonné en proie à mes appréhensions quant à mon agrégation au sein d'une entreprise locale. Mon anxiété ne fut atténuée qu'à la vue à travers les fenêtres du wagon, des premières lueurs matinales qui réussirent à s'imposer devant une pénombre persistante et me faire transparaître, somme toute, à l'approche de la ville blanche,  un lever de rideau insolite sur fond gris; un Bouregreg serein,  sommeillant, inondant une plantation luxuriante sur ses deux rives, et des lumières tamisées se dégageant des réverbères se mêlant aux premiers éclats du jour, caressant des chaussées rectilignes parfaitement tracées, et de loin, se profilèrent des vestiges médiévaux partiellement préservés marquant notre arrivée à la ville millénaire...A mon arrivée, une flânerie dans les dédales de la vieille médina de Rabat me fit découvrir, une fois de plus, à quel point cette cité médiévale est à la fois étrange et hétéroclite; de l'atmosphère sucrée par les senteurs des pâtisseries aux nuées des fumées se dégageant des brasiers, des petits romans des simples plumitifs aux grandes œuvres classiques des maisons d'éditions célèbres parvenues miraculeusement aux étalages des bouquinistes, des simples badauds habitués des lieux aux regards harceleurs aux simples passagers en quête de sensations, ou encore, des lumières changeantes au fil de la journée à celles des réverbères à la nuit tombante , tout un enchevêtrement confus et incohérent d'événements, d'objets, des couleurs, des lieux, des lumières  et des souvenirs me faisant apparaître l'ancienne cité de Rabat , à chacune de mes visites, dans ses aspects les plus singuliers…(déplacement à Rabat… suite et fin)

author: @Boug0014
text: The flag you were referring to, is the Amazigh Flag.  The symbol in the middle ⵣ is Aza or letter Z in Tifinagh or Amazigh scripts which is in the middle of the word AmaZigh and symbolizes the Amazigh people, the natives of North Africa.  The colors of the flag symbolizes the North African geography, Blue for the Mediterranean and the Atlantic, the green for the forests in the Atlas mountains and the yellow for the Sahara desert.

author: @artdirector9517
text: 👍 About casablanca city. for the new tourists, If you thinking visiting Casablanca one day, there are many things to visit other than the big mosque. For exemple Casablanca has the largest mall with a sea view in the world and the only one in the world as far as I know. It's called Morocco mall. In fact there are 3 malls with sea views lool the 2nd called Anfa Place, the 3rd called Marina Mall, it's refreshing, especialy for chilling. The coast is completely renovated with a beautiful view and all the time is empty and calm except Sunday. 2- The old imperial Habouss district is good to visit too. 3 - the Maarif district. But the most surprising thing is that between Casablanca city and between El Jadida city and El Oualidia city there are 180 km of wild beach inside civilisation haha 🙂 something that we couldn't have imagined, yes. Sidi Abed Beach for example. Good thing to hear especialy for Van life tourists. In short the most interesting and the easier thing that you can do especialy if you love walking is going from Anfa-place mall to Morocco-Mall 5 klm of cost views cafe, restaurant and much more. For exemple taking a chilly breakfast in the Marina-Mall taking lunch in the open-air Anfa-Place Mall and finshing the tour evening by making tour in the last and the bigest one. Iknow i' speaking a lot about the beach side because it's the right plan to running from cars tracffic of a big city like Casablanca. Keep going So good luck 👍

text: @whe@whereisgabri3641ree and if we are still alive and well by 2030 Rabat would have developed even more. The year that’s a critical milestone is 2040. In the MENA-region it can actually mitigate the youth unemployment rate and social unrest compared with other countries.

Otherwise, each god would have taken away what it has created, and some of them would have gained supremacy over others. Glory be to God, far beyond what they describe. The Knower of the hidden and the manifest. He is exalted, far above what they associate. (91-92 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah means the one and only God, the God of all prophets and creatures, the creator of the universe and mankind, and the Master of the Day of judgment, where our destiny, Hell or Paradise, is decided based on our faith and deeds in this trial life, and above all, Allah's mercy. 

Allah said in Surah Al-Ikhlas : In the name of God, the Gracious, the Merciful.
Say, “He is God, the One. God, the Absolute. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.” (1-4 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah said in Surah An-Nisa : O FOLLOWERS of the Gospel! Do not overstep the bounds [of truth] in your religious beliefs, and do not say of God anything but the truth. The Christ Jesus, son of Mary, was but God's Apostle - [the fulfilment of] His promise which He had conveyed unto Mary - and a soul created by Him. Believe, then, in God and His apostles, and do not say, "[God is] a trinity". Desist [from this assertion] for your own good. God is but One God; utterly remote is He, in His glory, from having a son: unto Him belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is on earth; and none is as worthy of trust as God. Never did the Christ feel too proud to be God's servant, nor do the angels who are near unto Him. And those who feel too proud to serve Him and glory in their arrogance [should know that on Judgment Day] He will gather them all unto Himself: (171-172 / Translated by Muhammad Asad).

Allah the Most Merciful said in Surah Ali-Imran : Behold, the only [true] religion in the sight of God is [man's] self-surrender unto Him; and those who were vouchsafed revelation aforetime took, out of mutual jealousy, to divergent views [on this point] only after knowledge [thereof] had come unto them. But as for him who denies the truth of God's messages - behold, God is swift in reckoning!
Thus, [O Prophet,] if they argue with thee, say, "I have surrendered my whole being unto God, and [so have] all who follow me!" - and ask those who have been vouchsafed revelation aforetime, as well as all unlettered people, "Have you [too] surrendered yourselves unto Him?" And if they surrender themselves unto Him, they are on the right path; but if they turn away - behold, thy duty is no more than to deliver the message: for God sees all that is in [the hearts of] His creatures.
Verily, as for those who deny the truth of God's messages, and slay the prophets against all right, and slay people who enjoin equity - announce unto them a grievous chastisement.
It is they whose works shall come to nought both in this world and in the life to come; and they shall have none to succour them.
(19-22 / Translated by Muhammad Asad)..

God said : Say, “We believe in Allah, and in what was revealed to us, and in what was revealed to Abraham, and Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob, and the Patriarchs, and in what was given to Moses and Jesus, and in what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We do not differentiate between any of them. And to Him, we surrender.”
(2:136 / Translated by Community)

Salam (Peace)

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

text: Marrakech is so amazing! We stayed in a Riad in the Medina and did excursions to the Atlas mountains (blew our mind, so beautiful!), Essouira and a quad tour through the palmeraie. You do need to keep in mind to haggle in the shops, and not fall for the monkey scams on the square or ' i'll show you the way' scams but other than that...such amazing people and such a beautiful culture!

author: @ArjunLSen
text: Marrakesh is boring, the people working in the tourism industry are patronising and unfriendly, and you can get really ill eating the food. It was a massive disappointment and the most overrated destination I've been to in recent years. I live in Spain and there's more historic buildings in your average Spanish town than in the city of Marrakesh. The whole place is a big sell and a big con.  Avoid Marrakesh and probably Morocco. Africa is a big place and there are a lot more interesting and better value places to visit.

author: @bravo2966
text:
@CelticMickTravels Sounds good. I'm torn for 2026 whether to use Amsterdam as a base, hire a car and visit a lot of WW2 sites over Northern Europe or do Marakech.
As the airport for Amsterdam is a 10 minute drive from my house and the Marakech airport would be London, a 5-6 hour drive for me, I'm leaning towards Amsterdam atm. From same local airport to Marakech it's like £400 more than the London price and has a layover...so a bit of a bugger whichever way I do it.

author: @CelticMickTravels
text: ​@bravo2966 Amsterdam is great as well

author: @worldwildhearts
text: When you arrive at Marrakech Airport, Moroccan immigration officers will ask for your passport and may inquire about the purpose of your visit and the duration of your stay. They might also ask for proof of onward travel, such as a return flight ticket, and sometimes the address of where you will be staying.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: I said in the video that I was overwhelmed with the noise, crowd and fellow tourists. I have a love-hate relationship with the market. And you have no right to say that we are dishonest in our video. We go out as early as possible to beat the crowd,  and heat and it is always quieter in the morning. It is your fault for going out late then complain if it’s crowded. You should know the culture of the places that you visit. We also gave tips in the video, which you would know, IF you watched and listened to be careful of scammers. We knew about that by watching other travel videos and reading blogs before going. Again, it was your fault for not preparing yourself mentally of all the possibilities. Tourists are there to learn their culture, not to change it. If you did not like your experience, you have no right to accuse us of being dishonest when we clearly mentioned everything. Next time, before you talk, you listen to everything we say and have better understanding. If you do not like the crowd, go in the mountains!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
It's Chano here answering your comment, I am from Spain and I grew up in a Christian family, in case you may think I am Muslim because I love travelling to Morocco and learning about their history and culture. 
There is not historic evidence about a church being located where the Koutoubia Mosque was built. But our Cathedral of Sevilla was built on top of a previous mosque, and the old minaret was transformed into the beautiful Giralda bell tower, which is considered the architectural sister of the Koutoubia Minaret. 
The Spanish Christians built many churches on top of mosques after the Reconquista of Al-Andalus, and the Arabs did the same before as well, building mosques on top of Roman temples and Visigothic churches. And the Romans did the same on top of other religions temples. It was very common to repurpose materials or sites from previous religious buildings.
If you try to spread misinformation in our content we will have to block you. We respect different religions and ideas, so please do not spam our comment section with hatred. Thanks.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Nassa.
Thanks a million for watching our video and for appreciating our work! 🙏🏽 We're very happy to read your positive feedback and very kind words. Comments like yours keep us motivated to continue working hard and to share more travel documentaries around the world in the future. 🎥💪🏽😊

Regarding the herbal infusion, we cannot remember the price, but it wasn't expensive at all, they said it was done with mint, but not sure if something else was included. We still have some crystals at home, we use them with very little quantity and put boiling water when we have very bad hay fever for example, it helps to open the nose and it feels refreshing in the throat too. We haven't drunk it anymore though, as it was too strong, and just inhaling the boiling water it is more than enough for us. 
The name they had on their sign is: Herboriste Des Amis, and they had the following phone number on the sign too: 024382076 (Morocco's international prefix: +212). Google Maps: https://share.google/KlQ2z5FO6dCXCQQfl
We advise you contact your doctor if you have some health issues before taking traditional medicines too. Hope that helps!

We wish you all the very best to you too, have a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️😊 Let us know if you have other questions in other videos, we are always happy to share information about travelling with our viewers. Best regards!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Thanks for watching. I think we have showed in the video that most local people were very friendly to us. Of course, like in all the big cities in the world, you need to have common sense. We have explained in the video about how to behave in the souks to avoid issues. Take the usual safety measures like the ones you would take visiting big cities like Rome, Paris or Madrid, just to mention some big cities we have visited in recent years, where you have to keep your belongings safe in crowded places. Do not believe scammers approaching you in the middle of the street. Do not stay alone late at night in areas where there are not many people. In our whole trip in Morocco we felt safe, you can watch the rest of our travel series to have a broader idea. We caught public buses (even at night), the train, we stayed after sunset and woke up for sunrise. Not a single issue. We met some wonderful people who just wanted to chat on the streets too. If someone suddenly offers you to bring you somewhere or to sell you something, in any country in the world, just don't trust them. If they are just curious and friendly, be open to a nice conversation. 🙂

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Thank you for watching our video!

We didn´t exchange money in Morocco, we have a Revolut card that allow us to withdraw money for free in other countries up to 200€ per month, and then after that we pay only 1€ commission for every withdrawal. But you have to find the proper ATMs or Banks in every country to avoid paying commissions there. In Morocco, we only used the Al Barid Bank ATMs and we never paid commission with them. I would advise you to find them in Google Maps in every city in advance to save their location, there are not that many and better be prepared with enough cash, but if you are only visiting Marrakech, you have an Al Barid Bank ATM at the airport in the departures area (walk to the right side immediately before exiting out the airport). Then you have 2 or 3 more Al Barid Bank ATMs in the Jemma el-Fnaa square just outside the Post Office building. Sometimes we could pay in Restaurants and Hotels directly with our Revolut debit card. They always have great currency exchange.

We often show dishes with meat as you can see in our video, but we remember that in almost every restaurant that we show in this video, they had vegetarian dishes as well, you can find easily falafel, vegetarian couscous or vegetarian tajine. The tacos can be found without meat as well and they often have soups and salads done with only vegetables. So I think Morocco is a good country for a vegetarian to try local cuisine as well. And don´t forget their desserts! ☺️

Hope our reply was useful. Wish you a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Kitiwan, 
Thank you very much for watching our Morocco Travel Series and for appreciating our work. We´re really glad to know that the videos have been useful for preparing your coming trip to Morocco. You will love it there for sure! 🇲🇦❤️😊
So, ladies can wear whatever they want in public, in fact, sometimes we saw some local young women showing their legs with modern outfits, and many tourists were wearing short dresses as well. But at the same time, Morocco is a muslim country and they have certain cultural traditions and I preferred to respect that during our visit. I had heard some women receiving bad comments for dressing too sexy while visiting Morocco, but I only received smiles & nice comments about my dresses and the locals were often happy to see me with certain clothes. My husband helped me to choose some dresses before we visited, because he had already been there and he also knew the best colours for matching with the architecture 😅
In December, it may be cold early in the morning and at night too, so be careful with that, specially in the north of the country and in the desert. The Moroccan sun is very strong during the day, so traditional long clothing is actually the best for the desert for example. 
Hope my answer helped you a little bit!
All the best and wish you have a wonderful trip there!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Hello there Tom! There are indeed scams but for us they are very obvious. Also it was a great help that we watched a lot of videos before going there so we already knew what to avoid. For example, a random man who says that the way is closed then he will guide us to the correct way. Then he will ask money when you will arrive to the place. Do not believe them and just say no thank you. A lot of the merchants will be very friendly to you too and ask where are you from to start a conversation. We just answered them politely but we kept walking to where we were headed. If you know are lost for example in the souks because it’s like a labyrinth, just ask a merchant and probably avoid asking a random guy in the street. We were fine. We avoided “rough” areas. We used common sense. They are not going to hurt you. Some of them are just trying to earn money. Just ignore or say thank you. We felt safe in general. A lot of them are very friendly too. We met a family who just wanted to speak and practice their English. We are still in contact with them and invited us to visit their town.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
About the second question, Sardinia and Morocco are totally different. It depends on what you want to experience.

Sardinia is for beautiful Mediterranean coasts, coastal towns, chill life, amazing well kept nature, friendly locals, good food.

Morocco if you want to experience North African culture, more lively chaotic but unique culture, good food, mix of African and European architecture. And by the way, Moroccan architecture is impeccable and incomparable. They have one of the best designs I’ve ever seen. As a matter of fact, I want a touch of Moroccan design in my future house. Haha!! 

So if you decide which ones you want to experience, choose one. If you have more questions, feel free to ask us. We have Sardinia videos too.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Also, be careful of other videos describing the scams in Morocco. We found them too exaggerated. We’re not saying that scamming doesn’t exist but sometimes they were too much. And I think it is becoming more and more famous through social media so they are more careful now too.

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: What I also found insulting is your partner’s comment on the pastries you got from Haj Belkabir. Saying “I wonder how old these are??!” Is just unacceptable and implies that being sold old pastries and getting conned is expected in Marrakech! These mistakes completely undermined your video, what could otherwise have been a great documentary due to its graphic quality.

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: While I appreciate the attention you gave Morocco and Marrakech, I was quite annoyed by the way you handled and reported the snake charmers’ situation. Holding 20 dirhams (the equivalent of $2) in your hands, like some sort of protective strategy to get the snake charmers to let you film them alone was insulting. Then, you went on to describe the interaction like some sort of primitive encounter (that was not expected how it unfolded, you said). They took your camera to film the cobra up close, and you ended up with a great shot!! That didn’t seem to redeem them in your eyes. As to putting a snake around your neck, that’s standard practice, and, I believe, you must have researched it prior to going. I don’t think you said that out of malice, yet your “ classic Western tourist approach” is insulting and reinforces the civilizational/colonial prejudices.

author: @jgvarea
text:
Greetings. 
The sweets you show at 9:15 are mass-produced and don't represent the exquisite Moroccan pastry.
They contain more peanuts than almonds and sugary syrups instead of honey, and the flours are usually of poor quality.
To enjoy true Moroccan pastries, you have to go to Pastiseria Le Gato in Marrakech, among many others; they're nothing like what you find in the souks.

author: @ThomasRebelo-s3e4n
text:
Marrakesh is like the NEw York of Morocco .  The Medina is full of tourists milling around and the Medina is a constant hum of mopeds and scooters buzzing up the middle.  She said occasional i would say constant.  

  anywhere but Marrakesh would be my suggestion.  Fez or Chefchouan for an authentic experience.   Found a hamman in Marrakesh near the Grand old Mosque.  The old islamic college worth a visit.  The Bahia Palace under construction so sights limited.  I agree with photography paradise.  stay away from snake charmers..big scam.
but remember:  ripping off tourist , or worst hurting or assaulting a tourist is jail time, no questions ask.   So no matter what they demamd from u they wont do anything to you.  Military dictatorships are great for tourists. 

Negotiating is their national sport.  You will get ripped off (relatively speaking) they are just better that you.  you will end up buying a carpet u didnt think you needed.  the best way is to sample a price for let us say a leather bag. go to 3 different shops to get a sample price then you will have an idea what the price would be .  offer half and be ready to walk away .  u gotta be kind of rude . 
as for food , go outside Medina and you will find a grill smell.  look for a big pot on counter , it means soup is available for 10 MAD.  
it is a great country ,  people are generally nice ....explore the towns outside marrakesh

author: @KristinasTravels
text:
Apologies, this was not our intention. 
The sweets joke was just comic relief, as we don’t have open air sweet stores in Australia, so it’s just something that came to mind and we had a little chuckle about. Of course we didn’t think they were actually old, otherwise we wouldn’t have eaten them. 

In regards to the train station, we actually had a couple of uncomfortable encounters onboard the train. One man tried to befriend us and asked many personal questions, only to be escorted out of our carriage as he didn’t have a valid ticket to be in our booth. We also had another 3 people be rowdy, eat greasy food and drink while on the lookout for the guard, only to be also kicked out of our carriage which also made us feel uncomfortable. So, these situations, while they can happen everywhere, and in any country, did happen to us first hand. We chose not to include these stories in our report. 

Thank you for watching all the same, we had a fabulous time in Morocco, but we were also very much out of our comfort zone at times, but this is just one experience, and our experience. I’m sure everyone has a different story to tell 🙂

author: @paullefko
text:
Rent a car; hire a driver; go see the real Morroco and avoid the tourist stuff. A car is about 400 a week a driver is 150 a day. Not only are you wasting time sitting on train, you’re missing out on cities and towns along the journey. And by taking “tours” you’re locked into an agenda and if you want to explore more, well you can’t. 
Cash is king there; credit cards are rarely used around the country. 

Honnêtement, la vidéo est sympa, mais elle ne rend pas justice à l'authenticité du Maroc. Si vous y retournez, sortez de votre zone de confort touristique. J'apprécie que vous ayez opté pour des visites guidées par des locaux ; trouvez maintenant un guide local qui vous emmènera découvrir des endroits hors des sentiers battus.

Real tips: if you see it a line of tourists to go in… yeah pass; 2. Restaurants- the more the ranking and tourists… yeah no ;go to mom and pops. 
Coffee is awesome; but do an expresso with an Americano with milk. Big boost in taste. 
Tea is meant to sipped slowly
Don’t eat with your left hand, Moroccans don’t necessarily use utensils.

Demandez aux gens de  prendre en photo; beaucoup refusent. Utiliser de gros appareils photo et du matériel vidéo est très mal vu ; vous avez probablement attiré beaucoup de regards à cause de votre équipement.

author: @artdirector9517
text: Nice vlog 👌Yeah Kingdom of Morocco is stuning as package, his landscapes is incredibley diverse. Sahara desert, oasis, woods, snow, rivers, two water fronts long ocean coastline and mediterranean golden sand. surf spot, mountains, old medinas, vibrant souks, festivals, colorful cities etc.. He has too a rich culture of food, music, hand craft, traditionnel clothing, home furnitur, interior design, tiles etc.. As you can see in Marrakech city as berber saharien or in Tanger as mauresque with some spanish archetecture touch, the city that contains the world's first United States embassy in the history. Also kingdom of Morocco is the first country to recognize USA in the world at 1777. We are so open as civilisation for exemple about the spanish influences, they were in the north of Morocco for almost 50 yeras but not problem becaus we were in their country for 800 years 😊 that's pretty much isn't ?! lool. We left the last city Grenada at january of 1492, Just a few month later, they discovered America at october of 92 by Columbus using our tools. they translated too our books left in the university of Cordoba the best university in the world at that time. Two years after Spain become the new bigest super power on earth with Portugal. Why not ?! nothing was missing 🙂. Just for information it's the empire of Morocco who built almost 60 % of spanish cities. for exemple Garnata city become Grenada, Ichbillia city become Sevilla, kortoba become Cordoba, Balancia become Valencia etc.. It's so easy isn't ! 😎 as it is for the 40 other cities. All this legacy of sciences has crossed borders of the Andalousie reached France then to England and Florence city in Italy by Sicily island to declare the edge of renaissance in europe. For information, the oldest university in the world called Al-Qarawiyyin in Fez city and while there were 400 Hamam (bath) in Sevilla city and oil-powered lamp-posts in her streets there were any one in Paris or London. also is the oldest kingdom systeme in the world too with japan .Any way we are brothers every one of us carries the torch of progress. For reference, Steve Jobs' father is Syrian but the most signifiant of all this is the first traveler in the history and the first who draw a map of the planet earth were too moroccans, They are Ibn Battuta and Al-Idrissi. So strange isn't ?! 🤔 especialy it was a right map even without satellite 😅. Any way last time Morocco become the hidden paradise of surfers, motobikers, caravaners, stylists, artist, photographers, and digital nomades 🤫 in short when god reduces the world in half millions klm he gives Mor-occo. Joy it as you can, life is too short than you think. Hey 🙂 nice video 👍 keep going 🙏 .'''.'.''.''.'.'

author: @artdirector9517
text: this country is safe you have just to : 1 - put your back-bag on your chest in the crowded souks. 2 - bargain is part of the culture so you can bargaining about every price, it's normal in souks, propos always the half price pretends to leave and wait for his reaction. 3 - if you get lost just ask kiosk owners or old age locals or their ki dss is better 😉 4 - it's so easy to rent a car, renting a car doesn't cost too much like 30 dollars. 5 - taxis is very sheap too from 1 dollar just ask the taxi driver to turn on the distance metter it's obligatory for him. 6 - if you look for an tasty first Tagine experience. the real Tagine is : is when cooked directly in Tagine pot in live, is when meat is sticky on honeyed form but better if it was cooked on coal. Some streets activities prices : Henee hand tatoe cost like 8 dollars. Tips for musicians like 2 dollars but if you film with them a dance, more. Tips for Sneaks charmer like 2 dollars but if you shoot a vlog video with them, more. Short Camel ride or pigmy horse ride from 5 dollars. the golden advice here is : fixed the price with each one of them before to make easier your travel. Just free you mind and joy it as you can. A great kingdom, beautiful country and a welcoming people good luck 👍 my purpose is avoiding problems and misunderstandings between tourists and locals because I feel sad when I see a vlog that deals with small incidents that don't even deserve to be anxious about them, especially when traveling. Tips (few money) is nothing compared to a moment of joy. life is too short to live it with anxiety. Peace and love 🙏 nice vlog 👍 keep going. '.'.'.'.''.''.'.'

text: I went to Tangier around 2004...and I didn't feel like it was "dangerous" at all. It definitely looks like it has changed though by looking at your video. More modern looking. We were the only American tourists there at the time, in the middle of the summer in July but now I see everyone is going now. It was like that in Spain too, like not that many tourists, and now I hear there's people everywhere.. And long lines into the attractions wow. I'm going back for the second time, next spring. By the way... Is there soap, water, and tissue in the bathrooms? Do the bathrooms even work, of you're able to find one? That's how it awash when we went around 2003-2004. Yess!!! I remember the fish market there!!

author: @SueHutton
text: I see this was made in 2010. Many big changes in Tangier road systems since then and there is a project to 'join up' Tangier and Malabata bays. The view will be amazing as cruise liners come into port. If anyone is up, that is. They tend to come in before dawn. The medina is just the same. Oh, and the clubs and bars along the corniche have all been demolished for a vision of a 'clean' Tangier which is family friendly. That area will also look on to a marina. The fishing port is being shunted sideways. They're supposed to be trying to keep the walls of the Kasbah but they look very precarious.

text: Spent 6 weeks in SF at Treasure Island in '69 when I went on active duty. I liked it a lot, but never went back. I was stationed at Pearl Harbor in '69-'70. Waikiki wasn't a very nice place even then. I always felt so sorry for the WWII vets who came back to visit with their wives. You could see the disappointment in their eyes that the '40s Waikiki was long gone. I never had the desire to go back. Went to NYC in 1973 when I was a trucker, absolutely no desire to go back to such a dirty, polluted, unfriendly hell hole.

text: I've been to more places on this list than I would care to admit and it's a pretty good list. The Trevi Fountain was the only tourist site I visited in Rome that made me think: "Why did I bother?" The Hollywood Walk of Fame is a garbage dump with the names of celebrities written on the pavement. Las Ramblas in Barcelona was so packed with people you could barely move. As for Mt. Rushmore, I visited there back in 1990 on a cross country drive with a friend. When we got to the viewing location, my friend said something that was EXACTLY what I was thinking: "Is there anyone who comes here who does not think: 'Is that all there is?'" I am surprised that nothing in Bangkok made the list. There are multiple candidates there, even though the city (and Thailand as a whole) is well worth visiting. And, despite the number of beaches mentioned in this list, I applaud you for not including Copacabana Beach in Rio. Yes it is over-touristed. But the beautiful Brazilian-built bodies on the beach make it all worth while.

Your wrong when it comes to Niagara falls on the🇨🇦 side. We go twice a year usually a week before spring break and in the fall. We meet up with adult kids and g'kids for a week. Stay at a Best Western Cairn every time. It's got a big indoor pool, two huge hot tubs, kids multi level play park all in the indoor garden courtyard(in February/ March) arcade . Most of the rooms are off the huge garden courtyard. The courtyard has multi seating with tables areas. You can have multi family pizza night And other group meals. Bringing in food from outside is not a big deal. With kids in the pool, adults at the seating area watching everything. And the grandparents Soaking in the tubs with a cool glass of wine from the bar. We can walk to the tourist Clifton Hills and down to the falls. Check out  the great cemetery from 1812.  Day trips to Niagara on the Lakes, all the wineries if your going in the fall. The great fall color EVERYWHERE. Only newbies hang out at the Clifton Hills.
Most of the families in the early spring and fall are three generations of family groups. Kids all hang out and parents hit the casinos in the evenings and the gparents get quality time with both. The place when we go are full of kids at the best western. Kids of all ages that all hang out together. Alot of local families in 100K radios are also in the motel getting a break from winter with the kids. I look forward to it every year.

author: @MrPlenty2026
text:
I disagree with Waikiki being number 1.  I was there for the first time last January and would definitely go again.  Sure, it's a tourist trap like many other destinations on this list.  But, imo those places that have rampant crime (like pickpockets) and aggressive street vendors/performers are the worst.  For example, I was in NYC for work and had Time Square on my top destination spot.  The neon lights from the skyscrapers are pretty cool if you're from somewhere that doesn't have any of that.  However, two minutes into taking it all in and capturing the moment with my phone Minnie Mouse grabbed my arm, then grabbed my phone and started posing with me for selfies.  Seeing how a photo of me and Minnie in that setting was going to be great to send to my family and friends I let her snap a few.   Then, seconds later I got flanked by Mickey and Elmo and they got into the selfies.  After a few snaps Minnie asked for money.  I asked how much...and she said $20....in a Hispanic accent.  I was there for the vibes so I reluctantly  handed her the $20.  Then she pointed to Mickey and Elmo and I don't know if she understood me or not but I said, "hell no, you can share that $20 with them".  After a few seconds of her being aggressive and trying to get me to pay each of them they can see I was turning into the Hulk and there was a police officer nearby so they scattered.  Not a good feeling when you almost had to fight with Minnie, Mickey, and Elmo trying to punk you for some selfie money.lol.  We were left alone at Waikiki the hole time there even though it was crowded.  Our AirBnB was within walking distance to the beach which took us through the fancy retailers which was nice.  The beach was busy but wasn't overcrowded like Lake Tahoe (smh) so all in all we had a great time.

I had a friend that got her phone stolen in Paris.  Another friend said that Bali is fine but the travel time is unbearable...that there are other tropical places that are faster to get to and not as expensive.  I've never been to Egypt but from all accounts that place should be higher on the list.

author: @living_gold5709
text: We just visited Marrakesh, Rabat, and Casablanca. No problems at all. There can be sensory overload in the high tourist areas but going in the Spring season (late March/early April) is a good time to experience Morocco with smaller crowds. Most importantly, we had excellent  guides licensed by the government. We weren’t harassed by street vendors or merchants. While looking in one shop, the merchant told me to come back without our guide. I chuckled and kept it moving. But when vendors see you are accompanied by a guide , they move on. However, if you want to exercise the art of bartering and improve your haggling skills, Marrakech is definitely the place to do it.

author: @Zile-j3u
text:
Although there is a lot of crime in South Africa, its generally concentrated in very specific spots of each city.

A lot of people exagerate with the "if you go there you will die ", thing.

It's more like "If you go there alone, and walk around long enough without anyone from there, there is a very high chance you are getting robbed. Possibly killed, tho this is far less likely." This also applies more at night than during the day, though it can 
But if you are there with a local nice person, who often you could just show up and find there, the odds of anything happening to you drop drastically.

Still, its non-zero, epsecially in the really bad parts with high gang activity. But in most of the areas, its greatly reduced, and infact now unlikley.

In most of the more public areas, they are completely safe.  But you will see barbed wire and electric fencing mainly to deter break ins at night or while you are at work.

Criminals from the rougher areas, especially addicts, might ocassionally venture out and see what they can steal.


A simple rule is:
If you see patchy grass or paint, probably not that safe of an area.
If you see shanty towns, I mean its obviously not safe then.
But if you see nice looking buildings or its a place with high civilian traffic like malls or something, then its probably quite safe.

So you dont have to be on the alert 24/7 if you know you are not in a particularly dangerous area, as you know you are pretty much safe.  But there are occasionally some small exceptions here and there, especially eith opertunistic crimes like grab and runs etc,  so most people just try to be a little bit cautious.
I.e dont walk around with your phone out, usually best to walk with someone else tho not necessarily required if you are male, and just generally be aware of your surroundings.

Although there are lots of criminals, there are far more nice, everyday south africans, who would be willing to help out. So dont stress too much if you are surrounded by them.

If they have a job, or are with family/friends and wearing clean clothes, chances are they are not the type of person to rob you in a public area with tons of other around. (Usually doesnt happen there anyway) I mean, a petrol attendant isnt gonna rob you when you know where he works! Plus he is willing to be working to make a living, so why would he need to rob you?
If there are 4 of me and my friends and only 1 shady looking guy standing there, chances are he isnt gonna try something. Etc.

Just think about stuff like that and you are pretty much safe.

author: @viking_rogaland
text:
I have lived for many years both in the Philippines and in Thailand. I came from MIndanao to Thailand 3 weeks ago. If you like to visit those islands ini the Philippines that have no reliable electrical grid, and you plan to go for only a couple of weeks, it sure is not a good time to go now. 
However, there are many places in the Philippines that have developed during the 12 years I have lived there.
It really depend on the place you want to go.
Me I am not an island hopper, I am a retiree that likes to stay put in a place where things work, and where I feel at home and safe. 
The area in Mindanao that I have lived in for 10 years (Davao del Sur and Davao Occidental) have places with very bad internet and blackouts all the time. But in Digos, where I have lived for 7 years, there is a great electrical grid with very rare brown outs as they call it. The water supply is great. It has beach and vicinity to Mount Apo with a very cool climate.'three Malls: 1 is top notch with cinema, the others are totally acceptable.
 
So if you are staying for long term, there is nothing stopping you from going there. The International fligths are not that expensive. I flew from Davao city via Manila with Philippine Airlines, to Bangkok, and I paid less than 200 usd including 30 kg check in luggage, just 3 weeks ago.
And my friend just booked a return ticket from Vietnam to Manila  a few days ago (he has been living for many years in Mindanao before), and he was surprised that the ticket was actually cheaper now than last time he took the same flight in January.

author: @mrc_nrd
text:
I believe the Philippines was already in crisis, even without any conflict in the Middle East. From what I know, the country has a low tourist return rate. People visit once, appreciate the friendly locals, the easy communication in excellent English, and the beautiful beaches—but there have always been downsides. People think twice about whether they want to deal with those disadvantages again after a great first trip, or if they’d rather go to countries that are easier to reach, more stable, and have better infrastructure.
I spent a month in Davao and some time in Manila. Manila isn't my favorite, but at least the infrastructure I needed was there. In Davao, we occasionally had bad internet, no water, or the occasional power outage. If you go further toward Zamboanga City, these problems increase. There are a few places with better facilities, but they are so crowded that you’d have to be a fan of mass tourism—all while dealing with poor general infrastructure and, in my view, quite high rental prices for short-term travelers. There’s no question you can live extremely cheaply as an expat, but as a tourist, I don't think it's the best value. Other countries are better suited for that.
As for the scams, I don't really mind them; they are everywhere. In Bangkok, taxis tried to drive without meters; in Cambodia, I was cheated during a currency exchange; and in Vietnam, my backpack was slashed in a crowd. There was this one incident (laughs): a heartbreaking scream from a young boy who reached into my bag and cut his hand badly on a kitchen knife I was carrying. Surprisingly, I’ve never had anything stolen in a hotel, but friends have—money just vanished from a locked safe. I’ve even dealt with credit card fraud in Vietnam. So, scams aren't unique to the Philippines.
However, there is much more begging compared to Thailand or Vietnam. And trike drivers, much like Tuk-Tuk drivers in Bangkok, love to quote astronomical prices. Then there was the earthquake—it was only a mild 3.0, but it still startled me. You just have to accept that when traveling in this region. I didn’t experience a typhoon, but there are many every year that eventually roar on toward Vietnam and Thailand.
It’s also getting harder for expats to find decent housing. If you have money and don't want to live on the poverty line like some expats do, you’ll realize how little quality you get for a lot of money. A friend of mine married a Filipina last year and was determined to emigrate. But the hurdles kept getting higher. A decent house for him, his wife, and daughter easily costs €300,000 or more—and that’s with poor infrastructure around Davao. They considered moving to BGC because of the safety and Western amenities, but now they’ve changed their minds completely: all three are moving to Germany. For a family of three, the costs in the Philippines aren't much lower than in Germany if you want to maintain a certain standard.
Of course, it depends on the individual. Some live very cheaply there because they don't need a €300k house or €3,000 a month. But for my friend, moving to Germany means saving on expensive flights and getting free schooling for his daughter. In five years, she could get German citizenship and one of the strongest passports in the world, opening up far more opportunities. It’s ironic: I want to get out of Germany, and they are moving there. In his situation, it has many advantages; in mine, not so much.

author: @milpitians5621
text:
Reading some of the comments for this video, I feel they are a little too negative on the Philippines compared to my personal experience. I have currently been here for two months and plan to stay another three. I will be the first one to admit that most of the country is too polluted with bad infrastructure. I've been to five cities already where this is the case. However, in Iloilo, I have found it to be relatively clean, the power stays on, and the traffic isn't too terrible. Are there transactional people who only treat you well because they view you as a rich foreigner? Absolutely. But I have also made friends with people who don't care about money. They make their own. So it really depends on where you hangout and who you make friend with. In terms of being ripped off, I haven't experienced that at all since I don't eat at the food stalls. Every restaurant I've been to has a menu with listed prices, and I've ALWAYS paid the price that was listed, nothing more. I also take Grab taxis everywhere, which charges you a predefined amount before you book the ride. Getting around the country can be a pain, but it hasn't been a dealbreaker for me yet. I was surprised I could get from Moalboal to Iloilo without having to board an airplane. Buses and ferries did the trick. The prices of goods are increasing, but they are still extremely low compared to what I pay in the United States.

The one area that is a big problem is the rising fuel costs, as noted in this video. I have noticed that Grab trips are costing more now. Airline tickets to get here are also insane. They've nearly doubled. Originally, I had planed to leave in May and comeback in the fall. However, with soaring ticket prices, I've decided to just stay for the summer, then leave for the US in the fall and not return until the new year. I am afraid of what this will do to tourism in the Philippines. The people here are hardworking and resilient. But in the end, that doesn't put food on the table. Hopefully, the war will end soon and prices will start to drop.

author: @100DOLLARCAMERA
text: You are correct:  Boracay Island is primarily powered via submarine and underground cabling connected to the Panay mainland grid, rather than having its own central power station. Electricity is supplied by the Aklan Electric Cooperative (AKELCO) and the National Grid Corporation of the Philippines (NGCP), traveling from the Nabas Substation on the mainland to Caticlan, where it crosses the channel through high-voltage submarine cables. To maintain the island's aesthetic and protect the lines from the elements, much of the distribution on the island itself is handled through underground cables. As of 2026, reliability has been significantly improved by the completion of the 138-kV Nabas-Caticlan-Boracay Transmission Line Project, which reinforced the connection to the mainland to prevent the frequent blackouts of the past. While the grid utilizes a mix of energy sources from the mainland, many resorts still maintain independent diesel generators as emergency backups.

author: @swizzthesecond
@mrc_nrd
text:
@swizzthesecond "Yeah, I’ve skipped Cebu so far because, for me, the negatives simply outweigh the positives. I’m in Da Nang now. I could take a direct flight from here to Cebu, but nope—in 10 days, I’m heading back to Bangkok, right into the low air quality of the burning season.
If I look for a small condo near IT Park, I’m expected to pay 1,000 USD a month for a 25 sqm room. I know a German expat living near Cebu in a townhouse for 200 USD a month, but as a short-term visitor, I can’t seem to find any good deals. Maybe I’m just too picky.
And fun fact: despite paying 800 USD a month for my condo in Davao, they charged me 200 Pesos every single day just to use the pool. I’ve never experienced anything like that in any other country. Sometimes it feels like everyone is just trying to milk me.
The Samal Island trip was the final straw: I wanted to take the ferry from Davao, but I had to pay to enter the harbor, then pay again just to enter the building to buy a ticket. Then they told me my 8 kg backpack was too large and demanded another 100 Pesos. I didn't want to hand over my bag with my laptop and camera, so they quickly became angry and rude. I thought, 'Okay, no Samal Island for me today,' turned around, and left. And honestly, with the news you read lately, you have to be careful—they might even arrest you if you lose your temper and call them 'scammers' in public."

"At the end of the day, I think it’s also just me. I’m simply not the right person for the Philippines; it’s too inconvenient for my lifestyle. Thailand and Vietnam are much easier for me to navigate. That said, Cebu is packed with expats, so there are clearly plenty of people who handle the local circumstances much better or adapt more easily than I ever could.
It’s a shame, honestly. People always talk about how friendly Thais are—and while they've rarely been unfriendly to me—being in the Philippines felt different. Aside from the scams, the Filipinos I spent more time with were, in my opinion, the friendliest Asians I’ve met so far. It was incredibly refreshing to be able to have real conversations again, and the visa process is so much easier. But everything else is just a huge deterrent for me. That’s why I still think about the Philippines; many things were truly great, but the daily struggle just isn't for me."

text: This is True...I been going to the Philippines for more then 8 years and tried different places and finally the past 3 years I settled in one place that is both clean, quiet, safe, and with out any scams or problems with foreigners.  San Fernando Pampanga.  Outside the city where the nicer condos are at can be very rewarding and peaceful...at least what I found.  Head over to crazy Angeles City for some crazy fun...and that's where the scams and corruption is.... Peace is out there in the Philippines just need to experiment and test out if you really like to go there.  Then don't bounce around the different places.  I will be there again have already paid my tickets 4 months ago so actually 7 months in advance.  I don't know what to expect now...guess I can update after.  So far my friends there say nothing is too drastic except the prices of gas, but I heard now there are electric cars, and just like Grab there is an app to order an electric car instead,  but lets see how that works...stay tune.

author: @lalanila1113
text:
Funny how your hate for this team—whether driven by jealousy, old grudges after losing to Morocco, or simple ignorance—has blurred your judgment. Let me point out a few facts.
First, regarding the accusations of corruption: aside from the fact that this is a media propaganda aimed at undermining the remarkable organization Morocco showed during the Africa Cup of Nations, I would simply ask for one solid piece of evidence.

Second, if you are all talking about justice, where was it when Senegal left the field for 15 minutes, applying pressure on the Moroccan team? Such behavior should have been sanctioned, potentially with yellow cards for the team, and even considered a forfeit.

Finally, let’s not forget the unacceptable behavior of some Senegalese supporters, who reportedly destroyed stadium seats and screens. There are also claims that Moroccan students in Senegal were harassed, with videos circulating online. At that point, it goes beyond football that's a whole anti Morocco propaganda , all because of 
long-standing grudges that some neighboring countries may hold toward Morocco.

author: @R9_ilfenomeno
text: It was the Senegalese Football Federation that announced the train's arrival at the station on social media and didn't bother to notify CAF to increase the number of security personnel; it was a premeditated dirty trick. Furthermore, the Senegalese federation traveled with its own cooks, so the poisoning claim is yet another piece of fake news, which the players have denied. Regarding the infamous disallowed goal, it's clear the referee blew his whistle well before the ball reached the Senegalese player. As soon as the referee saw the foul on Hakimi, he blew his whistle—a perfectly normal situation. The problem with this AFCON is the propaganda and fake news that began well before the competition and escalated throughout. There was a toxic atmosphere of general paranoia, and this was reflected in the events of the final. If Morocco had truly bribed the CAF, the referee would have had all the necessary legal elements in Morocco's favor to end the match and declare a victory for them after the Senegalese players left the pitch. We need to stop spreading fake news.

author: @ashaba07
text:
I’m a Moroccan fan, and you need to understand things. 
There is a deep rivalry between Morocco and Arab countries, especially those in North Africa, Morocco has become one of the strongest nations in Africa in terms of long-term football strategy, organization, and vision. 
In just four years, the national team achieved major international results, and many rivals realized they simply cannot keep up with Morocco’s progress.
Because of that, they keep exploiting the narrative that Morocco is “cursed” in the AFCON, since the team hasn’t won it for over 50 years. They know that once Morocco wins the AFCON again, it could dominate the competition for years and be considered the greatest national team in African football history.

This is why Algerian and Egyptian media, in particular, have made huge efforts through their outlets to criticize everything related to Morocco, their goal is to damage Morocco’s image and promote the idea that Morocco hosted the AFCON only to win it unfairly. Every decision that slightly favors Morocco (even a 60/40 situation) is exaggerated and used to push this narrative. They also know this strategy could weaken Morocco’s relationships with other strong teams, especially Nigeria and South Africa. 
Unfortunately for them, those teams acted with fair play and publicly acknowledged Morocco’s unprecedented level of organization.

At the same time, the Senegal coach, supported by an Algerian assistant and the Senegal FA president, who has personal hostility toward Fouzi Lekjaa after being blocked from becoming CAF president, put enormous pressure on Morocco over trivial issues. 
They complained about player protection during training, even though Senegal themselves publicly announced their arrival time and training location on Facebook.They accused Morocco of spying on them and avoid training in the moroccan football center, criticized hotels despite staying in some of the best facilities in Africa (just bcz the tanger is a touristic city and has a 9/10 hotel and moved to an administrative city that hasd 8/10 hotel in rabat, and turned logistical details into political drama.

On the pitch, after the referee correctly awarded a penalty to Brahim Díaz, Moroccan supporters living in Senegal were filmed hoping he would miss it, because they were reportedly threatened with death by Senegalese fans if Morocco scored. Senegal then escalated matters by threatening to damage Morocco’s image as a future 2030 World Cup host, even hinting at walking off the pitch, eventually, under pressure from higher authorities, Díaz missed the penalty.

In general, Morocco always brings its own logistics when playing abroad specifically to avoid criticizing host countries or harming their image. 
Morocco has hosted many national teams that lack proper stadiums to prevent them from losing by forfeit. 
Morocco has also lost many AFCON tournaments in different ways without ever blaming referees, organizers, or external factors.


The real issue is geography and mentality, Morocco doesn’t belong to the way African football politics work, and Africa doesn’t deserve Morocco. So when Morocco eventually wins the World Cup, don’t suddenly support us or claim us. Especially not when many African and Arab teams still struggle just to take a shot against European teams.
Dima maghreb

author: @Andalus4Ever
text:
Let’s look at it from a regulatory standpoint, because several key concepts are being misunderstood.

First: the argument that “the referee’s decision is final” is being misapplied.

Yes, under Law 5, the referee’s decisions during the match are final in terms of on-field play.

But that does not mean the result is immune from post-match disciplinary review.

That’s a completely separate layer of governance.

Now, the core issue here is straightforward: a team leaving the field of play without the referee’s permission constitutes a regulatory breach.

And the rules on that are not ambiguous:

- Leaving the field without authorization is considered a serious violation

- It can lead to a forfeit, regardless of what happens afterward

So the argument that “they didn’t leave the stadium, only the pitch” is irrelevant.

The obligation is to participate in the match on the field of play, not merely to remain somewhere inside the venue.

Now, here’s the key misunderstanding:

“The game restarted, so everything before that is erased.”

That is simply incorrect.

Football governance clearly distinguishes between:

- The sporting outcome on the field, and

- The regulatory outcome after review

A referee allowing play to continue does not nullify a prior breach of the rules.

Disciplinary bodies exist precisely to address situations where infractions occurred, regardless of how the match was managed in real time.

Let me put it in a simple analogy:

Imagine a marathon runner who takes a shortcut off the official route, then re-enters the race and crosses the finish line first. At the moment, officials might not stop them. The race “finishes.”

But once the violation is reviewed, the result is corrected — because the rules of the competition were broken.

The same principle applies here.

Finally, claims of “disgrace,” “illegality,” or “backroom decisions” are speculative and not substantiated by the regulatory framework. These decisions are made within established procedures, and they remain subject to appeal — including at CAS.

So the bottom line is this:

Emotions about fairness are understandable.
But they do not override the rules.

And the rules are clear:
if a team leaves the field of play without authorization, it exposes itself to disciplinary sanctions — including forfeiture — even if the match was later resumed.

That’s not opinion.
That’s the regulatory framework.

Dima Maghrib ❤️🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦❤

author: @LahlouAbde-f7j3n
text: The Senegalese national team displayed a blatant lack of respect towards the FIFA president, the CAF president, the tournament organizing committee, and the senior sports officials present at the stadium during the final match between Senegal and Morocco, the host country of the 2025 CAF World Cup in the Kingdom of Morocco. The players left the pitch for the locker room for approximately fifteen minutes. They may have showered, had a drink, or used their phones before returning to finish the match with utter insolence and disrespect. Their entry and exit from the pitch mid-match, without any legal basis, freely and without sanction, constitute the greatest disgrace in the history of world football. Furthermore, they provoked unrest and clashes between Senegalese supporters and Moroccan security forces, tarnishing what was arguably the best and most impressive tournament ever held in Africa. However, these actions were orchestrated and instigated by parties hostile to Morocco in order to sabotage Moroccan and continental celebrations. Therefore, severe sanctions are necessary, and the law must be enforced. This should serve as a lesson for the future of African and world football.

text: Senegal faces significant challenges in education and economic stability, with 87% of the population being illiterate. Among those under 30, 57% are either unemployed or underemployed, while over 60% experience malnutrition, food insecurity, or starvation. Despite the resilience and potential of the Senegalese people, the country has been hindered by corruption, with the last three presidents—Diouf, Wade, and Macky Sall—reportedly embezzling a staggering $340 billion. This misappropriation has contributed to Senegal's status as one of the 30 poorest countries in the world, despite its wealth of resources and potential for prosperity. To secure a better future, it is crucial to invest in education and infrastructure, particularly by building more schools to prepare the next generation beyond soccer.

author: @AntoinetteSarpong
text: Congrats! How often were you posting each day?

author: @Ohnoplata12
text:
​@AntoinetteSarpongmy rule for myself is don't go more than 2 days without a reel/post. Could I potentially grow more if I was posting multiple times a day, making multiple stories, going live, commenting on similar posts to mine as soon as I make a new post, and everything else all the gurus say? Yes probably. 

But, the reality Is that when I was doing that? I enjoyed it for all of the week or two and then it just turned me off or I'd become uninterested. Now I do three to five posts a week, I'm not worrying about thumbnails and cool editing. I'm just focusing on consistency. And I'm happy how it's slowly and authentically growing.

author: @AntoinetteSarpong
text: @Ohnoplata12 yeah, that makes sense! I’m a small creator as well and I’m finding the same cadence is what I can do consistently.

author: @Ohnoplata12
text: Update. Still going consistently. 125 new followers in the past month. Once again, nothing crazy but exactly what I'm looking for with the minimal amount of effort I'm putting in.

author: @islamisthetruewaytogod6812
text:
Hello ! May Allah protect and guide you to his light and happiness in this life and the hereafter, God bless, Ameen. Excuse me for giving a little presentation of Islam, because it is very misunderstood nowadays, especially on those « Antichrist's » times, where media and politics are mixed to distort history and truth. And terrorists (puppets of the Antichrist) who misinterpret verses, out of ignorance and political motivations, and take them out of historical context (just like radical atheists do by the way) don't help either. Thank you very much for your time.

First of all, if you have any questions regarding Islam and yourself, just open the Quran randomly, and you will find the answer in front of you, like a miracle and a sign from God.
	
Islam is an arabic word that means the Surrender to the One and Only God, our Creator, Protector, Provider, who gives us life and all that we have, we are safe and sound by his will and grace, we are His and to Him we return, and we have to thank him in this trial life by submitting to him by our free will, or later in the Day of judgment when it's too late to save our own skin. Islam was the original Religion descended to earth from heaven with Adam and Eve (peace and blessing be upon them) in the beginning of humanity. and was passed to people with the succession of the 124 000 prophets and 315 messengers of God to all nations and civilizations since, passing by Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ismaël, Joseph, Moses, Aaron, Joshua, David, Solomon and Jesus (Peace and blessing be upon them) during the history of mankind, the last replaces and completes the previous, until the succession of the last messenger of God fourteen centuries ago, Muhammad (Peace and blessing be upon him) to complete the noble morals of all mankind, to bring humans and jinns out of darkness into light, and to purify people's religion and belief from corruption and polytheism, and return it to purity and true monotheism, like it was in the times of the prophets (Peace and blessing be upon them).

Many Religions that we know nowadays, at their beginning were true and under Islam, initiated by one of the prophets of God, but their original teachings, history and scriptures have been corrupted over time with falsification and polytheism, or lost and replaced with false ones. That's why Islam is the only Religion accepted by God nowadays, which consists in bearing witness that there is no god besides Allah (God in Aramaic, the original language of Jesus and the Gospel), and that Muhammad is His servant and messenger, just like Jesus and Moses and others are His servants and messengers. Never a messenger of God said he was God or literally son of God, it was the people after him who changed the words of God and corrupted the Religion. God is unique and absolute, He does not need to have a family and sons or to associate anyone else with His kingdom, He can simply create whatever He wants, everything belongs to Him, and to Him everything will return. Allah said in Surah Al-Mu’minun : “God has never begotten a son, nor is there any god besides Him. Otherwise, each god would have taken away what it has created, and some of them would have gained supremacy over others. Glory be to God, far beyond what they describe. The Knower of the hidden and the manifest. He is exalted, far above what they associate. (91-92 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah means the one and only God, the God of all prophets and creatures, the creator of the universe and mankind, and the Master of the Day of judgment, where our destiny, Hell or Paradise, is decided based on our faith and deeds in this trial life, and above all, Allah's mercy. 

Allah said in Surah Al-Ikhlas : In the name of God, the Gracious, the Merciful.
Say, “He is God, the One. God, the Absolute. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.” (1-4 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah said in Surah An-Nisa : O FOLLOWERS of the Gospel! Do not overstep the bounds [of truth] in your religious beliefs, and do not say of God anything but the truth. The Christ Jesus, son of Mary, was but God's Apostle - [the fulfilment of] His promise which He had conveyed unto Mary - and a soul created by Him. Believe, then, in God and His apostles, and do not say, "[God is] a trinity". Desist [from this assertion] for your own good. God is but One God; utterly remote is He, in His glory, from having a son: unto Him belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is on earth; and none is as worthy of trust as God. Never did the Christ feel too proud to be God's servant, nor do the angels who are near unto Him. And those who feel too proud to serve Him and glory in their arrogance [should know that on Judgment Day] He will gather them all unto Himself: (171-172 / Translated by Muhammad Asad).

Allah the Most Merciful said in Surah Ali-Imran : Behold, the only [true] religion in the sight of God is [man's] self-surrender unto Him; and those who were vouchsafed revelation aforetime took, out of mutual jealousy, to divergent views [on this point] only after knowledge [thereof] had come unto them. But as for him who denies the truth of God's messages - behold, God is swift in reckoning!
Thus, [O Prophet,] if they argue with thee, say, "I have surrendered my whole being unto God, and [so have] all who follow me!" - and ask those who have been vouchsafed revelation aforetime, as well as all unlettered people, "Have you [too] surrendered yourselves unto Him?" And if they surrender themselves unto Him, they are on the right path; but if they turn away - behold, thy duty is no more than to deliver the message: for God sees all that is in [the hearts of] His creatures.
Verily, as for those who deny the truth of God's messages, and slay the prophets against all right, and slay people who enjoin equity - announce unto them a grievous chastisement.
It is they whose works shall come to nought both in this world and in the life to come; and they shall have none to succour them.
(19-22 / Translated by Muhammad Asad)..

God said : Say, “We believe in Allah, and in what was revealed to us, and in what was revealed to Abraham, and Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob, and the Patriarchs, and in what was given to Moses and Jesus, and in what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We do not differentiate between any of them. And to Him, we surrender.”
(2:136 / Translated by Community)

Salam (Peace) -----

author: @casawevrai4555
text:
This documentary only shows what is beautiful and positive! What about the negative side? What about the homeless, especially the children who are deprived of education due to the lack of schools in their villages?
What about the suffering of Moroccans with unemployment, poverty, and lack of healthcare due to the absence of hospitals,
Among them is human rights activist Saida Al-Alami, and the lawyer and former Minister of Rights Mohamed Ziane, who is 84 years old and is still imprisoned on fabricated charges.
What about torture prisons, and arrests for anyone who demands their legitimate rights and criticizes the policies of the tyrannical king?
Morocco is a beautiful country rich in its resources, but the tyrannical king and his repressive regime have taken control of everything and impoverished the Moroccan people.

More than 8 million Moroccans have left beautiful Morocco to live in Europe, and I am one of them, fleeing the oppression of the king and his police regime.

author: @islamisthetruewaytogod6812
text:
Hello ! May Allah protect and guide you to his light and happiness in this life and the hereafter, God bless, Ameen. Excuse me for giving a little presentation of Islam, because it is very misunderstood nowadays, especially on those « Antichrist's » times, where media and politics are mixed to distort history and truth. And terrorists (puppets of the Antichrist) who misinterpret verses, out of ignorance and political motivations, and take them out of historical context (just like radical atheists do by the way) don't help either. Thank you very much for your time.

First of all, if you have any questions regarding Islam and yourself, just open the Quran randomly, and you will find the answer in front of you, like a miracle and a sign from God.
	
Islam is an arabic word that means the Surrender to the One and Only God, our Creator, Protector, Provider, who gives us life and all that we have, we are safe and sound by his will and grace, we are His and to Him we return, and we have to thank him in this trial life by submitting to him by our free will, or later in the Day of judgment when it's too late to save our own skin. Islam was the original Religion descended to earth from heaven with Adam and Eve (peace and blessing be upon them) in the beginning of humanity. and was passed to people with the succession of the 124 000 prophets and 315 messengers of God to all nations and civilizations since, passing by Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ismaël, Joseph, Moses, Aaron, Joshua, David, Solomon and Jesus (Peace and blessing be upon them) during the history of mankind, the last replaces and completes the previous, until the succession of the last messenger of God fourteen centuries ago, Muhammad (Peace and blessing be upon him) to complete the noble morals of all mankind, to bring humans and jinns out of darkness into light, and to purify people's religion and belief from corruption and polytheism, and return it to purity and true monotheism, like it was in the times of the prophets (Peace and blessing be upon them).

Many Religions that we know nowadays, at their beginning were true and under Islam, initiated by one of the prophets of God, but their original teachings, history and scriptures have been corrupted over time with falsification and polytheism, or lost and replaced with false ones. That's why Islam is the only Religion accepted by God nowadays, which consists in bearing witness that there is no god besides Allah (God in Aramaic, the original language of Jesus and the Gospel), and that Muhammad is His servant and messenger, just like Jesus and Moses and others are His servants and messengers. Never a messenger of God said he was God or literally son of God, it was the people after him who changed the words of God and corrupted the Religion. God is unique and absolute, He does not need to have a family and sons or to associate anyone else with His kingdom, He can simply create whatever He wants, everything belongs to Him, and to Him everything will return. Allah said in Surah Al-Mu’minun : “God has never begotten a son, nor is there any god besides Him. Otherwise, each god would have taken away what it has created, and some of them would have gained supremacy over others. Glory be to God, far beyond what they describe. The Knower of the hidden and the manifest. He is exalted, far above what they associate. (91-92 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah means the one and only God, the God of all prophets and creatures, the creator of the universe and mankind, and the Master of the Day of judgment, where our destiny, Hell or Paradise, is decided based on our faith and deeds in this trial life, and above all, Allah's mercy. 

Allah said in Surah Al-Ikhlas : In the name of God, the Gracious, the Merciful.
Say, “He is God, the One. God, the Absolute. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.” (1-4 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah said in Surah An-Nisa : O FOLLOWERS of the Gospel! Do not overstep the bounds [of truth] in your religious beliefs, and do not say of God anything but the truth. The Christ Jesus, son of Mary, was but God's Apostle - [the fulfilment of] His promise which He had conveyed unto Mary - and a soul created by Him. Believe, then, in God and His apostles, and do not say, "[God is] a trinity". Desist [from this assertion] for your own good. God is but One God; utterly remote is He, in His glory, from having a son: unto Him belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is on earth; and none is as worthy of trust as God. Never did the Christ feel too proud to be God's servant, nor do the angels who are near unto Him. And those who feel too proud to serve Him and glory in their arrogance [should know that on Judgment Day] He will gather them all unto Himself: (171-172 / Translated by Muhammad Asad).

Allah the Most Merciful said in Surah Ali-Imran : Behold, the only [true] religion in the sight of God is [man's] self-surrender unto Him; and those who were vouchsafed revelation aforetime took, out of mutual jealousy, to divergent views [on this point] only after knowledge [thereof] had come unto them. But as for him who denies the truth of God's messages - behold, God is swift in reckoning!
Thus, [O Prophet,] if they argue with thee, say, "I have surrendered my whole being unto God, and [so have] all who follow me!" - and ask those who have been vouchsafed revelation aforetime, as well as all unlettered people, "Have you [too] surrendered yourselves unto Him?" And if they surrender themselves unto Him, they are on the right path; but if they turn away - behold, thy duty is no more than to deliver the message: for God sees all that is in [the hearts of] His creatures.
Verily, as for those who deny the truth of God's messages, and slay the prophets against all right, and slay people who enjoin equity - announce unto them a grievous chastisement.
It is they whose works shall come to nought both in this world and in the life to come; and they shall have none to succour them.
(19-22 / Translated by Muhammad Asad)..

God said : Say, “We believe in Allah, and in what was revealed to us, and in what was revealed to Abraham, and Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob, and the Patriarchs, and in what was given to Moses and Jesus, and in what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We do not differentiate between any of them. And to Him, we surrender.”
(2:136 / Translated by Community)

Salam (Peace) -----

author: @trueglobe66
text: Morocco is a land of incredible depth, where vibrant desert landscapes, historic medinas, and rich cultural traditions come together to create something truly timeless.

author: @trueglobe66
text: Morocco has a beauty that feels almost alive in the air, especially in its old medinas, where spice, stone, sunlight, and centuries of history blend together. What makes it even more special is that many of its historic cities were shaped by Amazigh, Arab, Andalusian, and African influences all at once, which is why the country feels so richly layered and unforgettable.
